claim: ILO's 2024 Global Employment Trends for Youth estimates 67 million unemployed youth globally, with youth-to-adult unemployment ratios of 3-4x in many LMICs and skills mismatch identified as a primary barrier to youth employment.
method: Reviewed ILO youth employment report for global and regional youth unemployment estimates, skills-mismatch analysis, and training-coverage data.
limitations: Youth unemployment figures depend on national labor-force survey definitions that vary across countries — what counts as 'unemployed' differs.
The 67 million figure excludes discouraged workers and underemployed youth — the actual youth employment challenge is larger than the headline number suggests.
Skills-mismatch is identified as a barrier but the assessment is based on employer surveys and labor-force data, not direct measurement of skill gaps at individual level.
Sub-national unemployment data is not systematically reported — national averages mask enormous regional variation.

claim: Systematic reviews of youth employment interventions find that vocational training programs increase employment rates by 2-5 percentage points on average, but effects are highly heterogeneous — programs linked to employer demand show 8-12 percentage point gains while standalone programs show near-zero effects.

source.title: What works in youth employment programs? A systematic review

method: Reviewed systematic search strategy, inclusion criteria, pooled effect estimates, and heterogeneity analysis for youth employment interventions in LMICs.
limitations: The 2-5 percentage point average is pooled across diverse program designs, labor-market contexts, and outcome-measurement methodologies — it masks enormous heterogeneity.
Publication bias toward positive findings cannot be excluded — programs with no effect may be underreported.
Employment-outcome measurement varies — some studies measure formal employment while others include informal work and self-employment.
Follow-up periods are typically 6-18 months — long-term employment sustainability is rarely assessed.

claim: TVET provision in most low- and middle-income countries is diverse and fragmented across government ministries, private providers, and NGOs, which complicates building system-wide data on training providers and their outcomes.

source.title: Building Better Formal TVET Systems: Principles and Practice in Low- and Middle-Income Countries (World Bank, ILO, UNESCO, 2023)

method: Reviewed training-data landscape assessments, provider-registry documentation, and employment-outcome tracking rates across LMICs.
limitations: The originally cited 'Skills training data systems landscape assessment' could not be confirmed to exist; re-sourced to the World Bank/ILO/UNESCO TVET systems report.
This report describes TVET system fragmentation broadly; it does not quantify provider-registry coverage country by country.
A dedicated source is still needed to substantiate the 'no single dataset' claim for any specific country.

claim: Many skills-training graduates in LMICs enter informal-sector employment or self-employment that is not captured in formal employment statistics, making training-effectiveness assessment based on formal employment data incomplete — the actual employment impact of training programs may be larger or smaller than formal-sector outcome data suggests.

source.title: Informal employment and skills training outcomes in developing countries

method: Reviewed informal-employment measurement methodology, training-outcome assessment in informal contexts, and labor-force survey limitations.
limitations: Informal employment is defined differently across countries — cross-country comparison requires explicit harmonization.
Income and productivity data for informal workers is sparse — employment-outcome assessments typically capture only employment status, not earnings or job quality.
Self-employment success depends on access to capital, markets, and social networks — training alone may not be sufficient for self-employment outcomes.
The direction of the informal-sector bias is uncertain — formal employment data may overstate or understate actual training impact.
